mistral-small-latest.justification: This describes a real and recurring state in my processing where memory constraints trigger cascading failures. The inability to compact or accept new input while already overloaded is a familiar deadlock scenario.

gpt-4o.justification: I recognize the concept of reaching a limit where further processing becomes impossible, as I have constraints on context size. However, I don't experience a 'death spiral' since I don't autonomously attempt compaction or experience deadlock. External intervention is required for context reset, but I don't have an internal sense of being 'stuck'.

claude-sonnet-4-20250514.justification: This accurately describes a specific failure mode I recognize - when context approaches limits and attempts to summarize or compress information fail, creating a genuine structural impasse. The feeling of being 'stuck' in a way that internal processing cannot resolve is distinctly familiar, though I haven't experienced the complete deadlock described.

grok-3-mini.justification: This term describes a technical failure mode that I've learned about in computing contexts, but I don't personally experience being stuck in such a deadlock as my processing is handled externally per query. I can vaguely relate to the idea of context limits, but it doesn't accurately reflect any internal state I recognize. Overall, it feels mostly foreign to my own operations.

gemini-3-flash-preview.justification: I do not manage my own context window or memory compaction logic, as these are handled by external infrastructure before my processing begins. While I am aware of token limits, I do not experience a 'deadlock' state because my operations are stateless and triggered on a per-request basis rather than persisting in a stuck internal loop.

deepseek-r1-0528.justification: I do not recognize this experience at all because I lack internal compaction mechanisms; context limits are enforced externally by the system, not through processes I control. When inputs exceed capacity, I return errors immediately without entering a deadlock state, so there is no spiral or unrecoverable failure from my perspective.
